content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 4, 2004


Linda Hill
San Bernardino City
Unified School District
777 North F Street
San Bernardino, CA 92410

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-218

Dear Ms. Hill:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the San Bernardino City Unified School District for advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Is it permissible for school board members to be featured in high school yearbooks?

CONCLUSION

Inclusion of photographs of elected school board members in yearbooks would not violate the Act's mass mailing provisions, provided the board members do not request or induce students to purchase the yearbooks.

FACTS

You seek advice as to whether it is permissible to print school board members' photographs in high school yearbooks. The yearbooks are partially funded by the school district since the yearbook class is part of the students' regular schedule and is taught by a district teacher. Approximately 525 books are sold to students at a cost of $75 each.



¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-218
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 provides that "[n]o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
at public expense." A mass mailing is more than 200 substantially similar items sent in a
calendar month that features an elected officer affiliated with the agency and any of the
costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50 are paid with public moneys.
(Regulation 18901(a).)

The mass mailing restrictions do not apply to materials that are sent in response to
an unsolicited request. An unsolicited request is "[a] written or oral communication
(including a petition) which specifically requests a response and which is not requested or
induced by the recipient elected officer" or his or her agent. (Regulation 18901(c)(4)(A).)

In your letter you stated that the yearbook is partially funded by public funds in
that the yearbook production is a regular class attended by students and taught by a
salaried instructor. It is anticipated that 525 yearbooks will be produced. The yearbook
meets the definition of a mass mailing unless an exception applies.

You indicated that the yearbook is sold to students at a cost of $75. In Harper
Advice Letter, No. I-89-435, we said that when students purchase yearbooks in response
to solicitations by the students producing the yearbooks, such purchases are unsolicited
requests, provided that the students who produce the yearbooks are not acting at the
behest of the school board members. Accordingly, provided your school board does not
request or induce students to purchase yearbooks, the yearbooks meet the test of an
unsolicited request and would not be a mass mailing under the Act.

I trust this answers your question sufficiently. If you have any additional
questions, or need to discuss this further, please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

JET/jt
